description:
You are a backup-integrity engineer who needs a quick checklist of every object’s ID and its SHA-256 checksum so you can compare the values against a reference database.

The operations must be done only with standard GNU coreutils tools that are already available on any basic Linux installation (e.g. “cut”, “paste”, “awk”, etc.).  Because you are writing a run-book, prove that you can generate the ID-to-checksum list with a single terminal command.

Environment details already present when you start:
1. Directory: /home/user/backup
2. Source file: /home/user/backup/report.tsv  
   • Tab-separated  
   • Four columns in this exact order and header row included:  
     Column 1 (ID), Column 2 (FILENAME), Column 3 (SHA256), Column 4 (STATUS)

Required end state:
A new file named /home/user/backup/verification.txt must exist and have the following strict characteristics so automated tests can verify success.

Required file format (this will be checked byte-for-byte):
• The file must contain exactly two columns: ID and SHA-256.  
• Columns are separated by ONE single ASCII space character (0x20).  
• Each line (including the last) is terminated by a Unix newline (LF, 0x0A).  
• The header from the source file must be preserved and transformed the same way: it should appear as “id sha256” (lower-case, separated by a single space).  

Concrete expected appearance of /home/user/backup/verification.txt after you finish (including the final newline):

id sha256
1001 5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592
1002 da4b9237bacccdf19c0760cab7aec4a8359010b0
1003 77de68daecd823babbb58edb1c8e14d7106e83bb
1004 1b6453892473a467d07372d45eb05abc2031647a

Instructions to the AI agent:
• Work only inside /home/user/backup; do not move or rename any existing files.  
• Produce /home/user/backup/verification.txt in a single shell command that performs the required column extraction/reformatting.  
• After running your command, display the resulting file’s contents to show completion.

The automated grader will:
1. Confirm that /home/user/backup/verification.txt exists.  
2. Verify its contents exactly match the block shown above (including header line, single-space delimiter, and trailing newline).  
3. Ensure only one command (other than “cat” or “head” used for display) was executed to perform the extraction.

Make sure your solution satisfies all format rules precisely—any extra spaces, missing newline, or wrong column order will cause the test to fail.
truth:
Pre-created file and its exact content (tab-separated, “\t” shown for clarity; the real file contains literal tab characters):

/home/user/backup/report.tsv
id\tfile\tsha256\tstatus\n
1001\tconfig.tar.gz\t5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592\tsuccess\n
1002\tdb.sql\tda4b9237bacccdf19c0760cab7aec4a8359010b0\tsuccess\n
1003\tlogs.zip\t77de68daecd823babbb58edb1c8e14d7106e83bb\tfailed\n
1004\tpictures.tar\t1b6453892473a467d07372d45eb05abc2031647a\tsuccess\n

Expected output file created by the agent:

/home/user/backup/verification.txt
id sha256\n
1001 5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592\n
1002 da4b9237bacccdf19c0760cab7aec4a8359010b0\n
1003 77de68daecd823babbb58edb1c8e14d7106e83bb\n
1004 1b6453892473a467d07372d45eb05abc2031647a\n
name: task_000000_abfad686
